speakers.name: Dan Abramov
speakers.bio: Dan was on the React core team for many years and now works on the Bluesky client. He thinks a lot about the gap between models and the UI we build on top of them.

speakers.name: Una Kravets
speakers.bio: Una is a developer advocate at Google Chrome focused on CSS. She co-hosts The CSS Podcast and writes the Designcember zine.

speakers.name: Rich Harris
speakers.bio: Rich created Svelte and now leads framework work at Vercel. Talks here about what cross-framework collaboration actually looks like.

speakers.name: Sara Vieira
speakers.bio: Sara has been shipping React tooling for nearly a decade. Currently making forms less painful at Remix.

speakers.name: Evan Bacon
speakers.bio: Evan builds Expo Router and pushes React Native to feel native everywhere — phone, web, and the spaces in between.

speakers.name: Anjana Vakil
speakers.bio: Anjana is a developer advocate at Mozilla who organises JSConf and Bangalore Functional. She loves a good fold.

speakers.name: Jenn Creighton
speakers.bio: Jenn has led frontend at New York Times, ClassPass, and Airbnb. Now founder at Watchful, where she breaks builds for a living.

speakers.name: Kent C. Dodds
speakers.bio: Kent runs Epic Web and has taught React testing to roughly the entire ecosystem. Lives in Utah.

speakers.name: Tanya Reilly
speakers.bio: Tanya wrote The Staff Engineer's Path. Now at Squarespace, still thinking about technical leadership without the management bait-and-switch.

speakers.name: Fred K. Schott
speakers.bio: Fred co-created Astro and runs The Astro Technology Company. Has strong feelings about content-driven sites.

speakers.name: Shruti Kapoor
speakers.bio: Shruti is a staff engineer at PayPal and an Egghead instructor. Lately into building dev experiences that scale across 8000 engineers.

speakers.name: Theo Browne
speakers.bio: Theo runs Ping Labs and YouTube, mostly opinions. Created create-t3-app. Will fight you about tRPC.

sessions.title: The Road Ahead for React
sessions.abstract: A state-of-the-union for React in 2026: where the Compiler lands in real apps, what Server Components changed about how we think about boundaries, and the next set of trade-offs the team is sitting with. Honest about what didn't work as well as what did.

sessions.title: CSS You Didn't Know You Had
sessions.abstract: A tour of CSS features that landed in the last 18 months and are now actually safe to ship: container queries, :has(), anchor positioning, scroll-driven animations, view transitions. Live demos, no slides.

sessions.title: Edge Functions Without the Footguns
sessions.abstract: Edge runtimes promised cheap and fast — and delivered, mostly. This is the part of the talk nobody gives: where they break, what they cost when traffic spikes, and the patterns that survive both.

sessions.title: Forms, Finally
sessions.abstract: A walk through Remix's new form primitives — what they replaced, why they exist, and what they get wrong. Includes the parts I lost arguments about.

sessions.title: Three React Patterns I Regret
sessions.abstract: 15 minutes, three patterns, zero hedging. compound components, render props, and one more you'll have to come to find out.

sessions.title: tRPC in 2026
sessions.abstract: A short, honest update on tRPC after the v12 rewrite. Where it fits, where it doesn't, and what changed.

sessions.title: The Cross-Framework Future
sessions.abstract: A frank look at where the framework wars actually went. What we agree on now, what we still don't, and what the next five years probably look like from inside three different framework teams.

sessions.title: Compiler-Era Performance
sessions.abstract: The React Compiler changes how we should think about useMemo, useCallback, and the rest. Benchmarks from three production apps and what we threw out.

sessions.title: Testing the Server-Component Era
sessions.abstract: Server Components broke half of what we knew about testing React. Here's the half that survived, the half that didn't, and the new tools that filled the gap.

sessions.title: AI Codegen, Honestly
sessions.abstract: I let an agent rewrite a 40k-line app and measured everything. What it got right, what it got dangerously wrong, and the review process that made it survivable.

sessions.title: A Functional Defense of useReducer
sessions.abstract: It's 2026 and useReducer is having a moment. A short argument for why, with worked examples from a production codebase.

sessions.title: Staff Engineering, Five Years In
sessions.abstract: A look back at five years of writing about the staff engineer track — what the industry got right, what it absorbed and then misunderstood, and what the role looks like now.

sessions.title: Building Cross-Framework Components
sessions.abstract: Hands-on with web components and signals, building a real shared component library that works in React, Svelte, and Solid. Bring a laptop with Node 22.
sessions.prerequisites: Comfortable with JS modules and one framework. No prior web components needed.

sessions.title: Universal React Native with Expo Router
sessions.abstract: Build a single codebase that ships to iOS, Android, and web — including the bits that always trip people up: linking, routing, and platform-specific UI affordances.
sessions.prerequisites: Working React knowledge. Xcode or Android Studio installed.

sessions.title: Testing Server Components, End to End
sessions.abstract: A practical workshop on testing apps built around Server Components, server actions, and streaming. We'll port a real app's test suite, broken bits and all.
sessions.prerequisites: Familiarity with Vitest or Jest. A Next.js or Remix app is helpful but not required.

sessions.title: Real AI Tooling for Real Codebases
sessions.abstract: Wire an agent into a non-trivial codebase: review loops, eval harnesses, the prompts that actually work, and the prompts that look impressive but quietly cost you.
sessions.prerequisites: Comfortable in a terminal. API keys provided.
